title: Psalm 22 - Salvation in Times of Great Distress

sections.opening: Sit, my dear, we have reached the final lesson in the Psalms course, and Psalm 22 is a chapter that has accompanied me for many years. This is a chapter that begins with the most painful words there are - "My God, my God, why have You forsaken me" - and ends with a promise of complete redemption. I remember how I turned to it during the hardest times in my life, when the world seemed completely dark and I didn't know how to continue. In this psalm, King David teaches us that even at the lowest moment, when we feel completely abandoned, God is already preparing our salvation. This is a psalm that occurs in the month of Adar, the month of miracles, and just as in Purim everything turned from trouble to joy, so too this psalm is about transformation - from distress to redemption.
sections.theoryA:
How does Psalm 22 save from great distress?

Psalm 22 is called "salvation in times of great distress" because it begins from the darkest place and reaches the greatest light. The words "My God, my God, why have You forsaken me" - this is the cry of someone who has reached rock bottom, feels that God has completely disappeared, and has no strength to continue. But this is exactly the place where salvation begins.

The special power of this psalm is against fear and great distress, and rescue from danger. Tradition tells us that whoever recites this psalm during a crisis merits swift salvation, because they connect to the power of faith even in the deepest darkness. This prayer moves us from a state of despair to a state of expectation for redemption.

Psalm 22 is special because it is connected to the month of Adar, the month of Pisces. Just as fish live in deep waters and still find their way, so too someone in the greatest distress can find a way out. The sages said that in Adar it is "turned around" - and there this psalm prepares us to receive transformation.

The secret of the psalm is the transition from personal prayer to collective prayer. David begins with "My God" - a personal connection, moves to "God" - recognition of greatness, and ends with "they will tell the people yet to be born that He has done this" - a vision of an entire nation meriting redemption. This teaches that our personal distress is part of something greater.

practice1.instruction: Write on paper a situation or fear that accompanies you now. First write the situation and your feelings about it. Then read Psalm 22 slowly, and listen to how the words connect to what you are experiencing. Finish by writing one sentence about the hope you find in this psalm.

sections.theoryB:
What is the connection between Psalm 22 and the future redemption?

Psalm 22 is not only about personal salvation - it is a psalm about the complete redemption of the people of Israel and the entire world. The verses at the end of the psalm speak of "a seed shall serve Him", "it shall be told to the generation", "they shall come and declare His righteousness" - this is a vision of a future where the entire world will recognize God.

The sages saw in this psalm a hint to the final redemption. The transition from "You are far from my salvation" to "The Lord is near to all who call upon Him" - this is the transition from exile to redemption. Just as David merits recognition and salvation at the end of the psalm, so too the people of Israel will merit the recognition of the entire world in their righteousness.

The connection to the month of Adar is important here - in Purim we saw how everything turned around within a short time. Haman was at the height of his power and within months was hanged on the tree he prepared for Mordecai. So too the redemption will come - specifically from within the deepest darkness. The greatest distress is the sign that salvation is near.

On a practical level, the psalm teaches us that even in the most difficult personal situation, we are part of a greater plan. Our suffering is not meaningless - it is part of the path to bring redemption to the world. When we recite Psalm 22, we connect to all the souls who have experienced distress and to all those who will still experience it, creating tremendous spiritual power.

practice2.instruction: Standing before a mirror or window, say aloud the verse "For He has not despised nor abhorred the affliction of the afflicted, neither has He hidden His face from him; but when he cried unto Him, He heard." Repeat the verse until you feel how the words enter you and change something inside. This is an exercise to internalize that God does not ignore our cry even when He seems distant.

sections.deepDive:
The Kabbalistic Secret of Psalm 22

Rabbi Nachman of Breslov revealed a deep secret about Psalm 22: he said this is the psalm that corrects the "fall of consciousness" - the state where a person loses the feeling that God exists. The words "My God, my God, why have You forsaken me" is not just a cry of pain, it is a spiritual correction of the most difficult state a soul can experience.

In Kabbalah, the greatest darkness precedes the greatest light. Psalm 22 represents the moment when the soul descends to the greatest depth in order to raise from there sparks of holiness that cannot be reached otherwise. This is the secret of "descent for the purpose of ascent" - sometimes one must experience the deepest darkness in order to recognize the true light.

The sages of the Zohar said that Psalm 22 connects to the Sefirah of Malchut during its exile. Malchut, which represents the Shekhinah, experiences exile with the people of Israel, and Psalm 22 is its voice. When we recite this psalm, we return the Shekhinah home, from above and below.

questions.question: Which month is appropriate for Psalm 22 according to tradition?
questions.explanation: Psalm 22 belongs to the month of Adar, the month of Pisces, where it is "turned around" - distress turns to joy as in Purim.

questions.question: What is the opening of Psalm 22?
options: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want
My God, my God, why have You forsaken me
Save, Lord, for the godly man ceases
questions.explanation: Psalm 22 opens with the painful words "My God, my God, why have You forsaken me" - a cry from within great distress.

questions.question: What is the special power of Psalm 22?
options: Salvation in times of great distress
questions.explanation: Psalm 22 is designated for salvation in times of great distress, against fear and danger, and rescue from difficult situations.

questions.question: How does Psalm 22 end?
options: In a vision of world redemption
questions.explanation: Psalm 22 ends with a vision of world redemption - "they will tell the people yet to be born that He has done this" - the entire world will recognize God.

questions.question: According to Rabbi Nachman, what does Psalm 22 correct?
questions.explanation: Rabbi Nachman of Breslov said that Psalm 22 corrects the 'fall of consciousness' - the state where a person loses the feeling that God exists.

closing.summary: We have completed the journey through Psalms, and Psalm 22 is the perfect conclusion. We learned that specifically in the deepest darkness lies the power to illuminate the world, and that no distress is eternal. King David's prayer teaches us that it's permitted to cry out, permitted to be angry, permitted to feel abandoned - and even within that to maintain the thin thread of faith.
closing.wisdom: From the deepest darkness grows the brightest light - this is the law of spiritual nature.
closing.nextPreview: Now, with all the tools of Psalms in hand, you are ready to begin your journey in other worlds within Maktuba. The light you discovered here will accompany you on whatever path you choose to walk.

faq.question: When is it good to recite Psalm 22?
faq.answer: Psalm 22 is especially intended for difficult times - illness, financial crisis, great distress, or when feeling that God is distant. It is also particularly suitable during the month of Adar.

faq.question: Is Psalm 22 only suitable for those in distress?
faq.answer: No. Psalm 22 strengthens faith even in good times, and prepares us mentally for difficult situations. It also connects us to the vision of redemption.

faq.question: How is Psalm 22 connected to Purim?
faq.answer: Psalm 22 belongs to the month of Adar when Purim occurs. Just as in Purim everything turned from distress to joy, so Psalm 22 promises that every distress will turn to salvation.

faq.question: What is the difference between Psalm 22 and other psalms of distress?
faq.answer: Psalm 22 is special because it begins from the darkest point ("why have You forsaken me") and reaches the most illuminated vision of world redemption. This is the most complete journey from darkness to light.

crossReferences.label: 🌙 The Moon Card - Journey in Darkness

seo.title: Psalm 22 - Salvation in Great Distress and Light in the Deepest Darkness
seo.description: Psalm 22 begins with 'My God, my God, why have You forsaken me' and ends with a vision of redemption. Learn how to use it in distress, its connection to the month of Adar and future redemption
